dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for an unknown problem/person down.
Hi, I'm Jamie, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's happening here?
She was alternating between being really agitated and hitting her head, and now she's just lying here unresponsive.
Let me check her responsiveness. She is unresponsive to verbal or physical stimuli, GCS is 4.
No eye opening observed. She is completely unresponsive.
No motor response when physically touched or stimulated during examination.
Airway is patent with no signs of obstruction, and she is being ventilated via BVM using high-flow oxygen.
Initial respiratory rate is slow, at 10 per minute with labored breathing, and equal bilateral chest rise.
Initial pulse is strong, 116 beats per minute, with regular rhythm.
Initial respiration rate is 10, but it increased to 23 during the assessment, with continued labored breathing.
Initial blood pressure was unobtainable, but later readings showed 124/63 and then 124/68.
SpO₂ was undetectable early but improved to 90% and later 98% with oxygen support.
Performed 4-lead EKG monitoring and noted sinus tachycardia on rhythm strip.
Pupils are 2 mm in size and non-reactive bilaterally. She is not oriented at this time.
Skin is warm, dry, with capillary refill under two seconds. No signs of trauma or skin compromise.
No discharge, bleeding, or obstruction noted in the oral cavity or nasal passages.
Equal bilateral chest movement observed. Respiratory effort is labored but rhythmic.
No deformities, angulation, or bleeding observed in the extremities at this point.

She has past medical history including Bipolar Disorder, Major Depression, and reported substance use with cocaine and opioids. You mentioned she's allergic to penicillin. Any new symptoms or changes today?
She was acting strangely, then became unresponsive. She said nothing about chest pain, dizziness, or abdominal issues.
Do you know when she last had anything to eat or drink?
I don't know exactly. She was alone and only came out after we knocked. It was strange behavior.
We are now checking her blood glucose level for further baseline assessment. Initial result is 74 mg/dL.
We are taking her temperature. The patient's temperature is 98.3°F, which is within the normal range.
We initiated ventilation using BVM at 15 LPM to assist her breathing. We keep monitoring her vitals and neurological status.
We performed ETCO2 capnography and got a reading of 56.
We are applying soft restraints due to her combative and erratic behavior to ensure safety during transport to the ED.
Her GCS improved to 14 en route, meaning she is now A&Ox3/4 with some confusion.
She shows normal motor and sensory function in all four extremities.
The patient is now secured to the stretcher and safely placed in the ambulance for transport to VCU ED.
We have contacted VCU ED and are en route. She is alert and oriented to three out of four elements now.
She is not speaking coherently, but she does respond to loud stimulation with some eye movement.
Her airway remains patent, and we're keeping it open with high-flow oxygen.
Strong regular bilateral radial pulses are currently present.
She is taking Seroquel, Clonidine, Phenergan, Mylanta, and Phenobarbital. No new medications were reported today.
No jugular venous distention observed during the head and neck assessment.
She is disoriented but responding to commands and some familiar stimuli during transport.
Her current respiratory rate is 22 per minute with labored effort, still showing some improvement.
Maintaining 4-lead EKG monitoring, currently reading sinus tachycardia again.
We are following the medical altered mental status protocol, number 3-15, based on field findings.
Her abdomen is soft without tenderness or distention, and bowel sounds are normal.
Skin turgor is normal, and no rashes or bite marks were found.
Hearing appears grossly intact. Ears appear clear, and nasal airways are patent.
She denies pain or nausea. No guarding or rigidity noted.
Both lower and upper extremities show normal range of motion with no angulation noted.
Her SpO₂ remains at 98% on high-flow oxygen.
The patient is en route to VCU ED with continuous monitoring for further evaluation and treatment.
She is allergic to penicillin. No recent reactions were reported.
No genital bleeding or discomfort reported. Vaginal or rectal exam not performed.
No signs of nasal flaring or oral secretions, and the trachea is midline and stable.
She is not showing nuchal rigidity or signs of neck trauma.
No airway positional changes or soft tissue swelling observed at this time.
Her systolic blood pressures are stable in the 124 mmHg range en route.
Her medical history includes Bipolar Disorder and Major Depression.
Breath sounds are normal and equal on both sides. No adventitious sounds heard.
We are transporting her in a supine position with soft restraints secured.
No signs of swelling, warmth, or cyanosis observed in the extremities.
No obvious spinal abnormalities or vertebral column fractures reported at this stage.
We will repeat the blood glucose later if needed.
She is maintaining a regular pulse of about 120 beats per minute with consistent rhythm.
We have maintained continuous observation and administered only oxygen at this point.
She later shows improved orientation, now A&Ox3 (to place and time).
ED was notified before departure and is prepared for her arrival.
Chest rise is equal and symmetric, without signs of paradoxical movement.
No tachycardic or delayed capillary refill observed.
She is showing some agitation but less combative, responding to verbal directions more clearly.
All actions are per protocol 3-15, as signed out by the PCP en route.
EKG was consistent with sinus rhythm again with no acute ST changes noted.
The patient is now stable and showing improved neurological function en route to the ED.
She exhibited erratic and agitated behavior but appears less agitated now.
Patient was carefully lifted and placed on the stretcher in a supine position.
Breathing is more controlled and less labored compared to initial findings.
Correct posture and no signs of use deficit noticed in any limb.
We are updating the patient en route so she's aware of what's happening during transport.
She's properly restrained, but not overly restricted-again, per protocol and PCP's clearance.
All notes and vitals are documented in the ePCR, and we are ready for handoff at VCU ED.
Transfer of care has been completed to the hospital nurses on arrival. No complications during transport.
Pressure points and physical landmarks are intact without signs of trauma.
No suprapubic masses or bulges during the abdominal check.
Patient is able to move all four extremities with normal mobility.
She is clean and appears coherent despite early unresponsiveness and bizarre activity.
During transport her behavior is now more manageable with verbal security support.
No medications were administered aside from high-flow oxygen per protocol.
Noted early AMS reversed to some extent, but mental alertness still fluctuates.
All signs look good for VCU ED as our destination.
We kept her in supine position per her initial unresponsive state and field findings.
No other drug or food allergies were reported at this time.
Stretcher locks and restraints are fully engaged to prevent any mid-transport issues.
Pelvis is stable and without abnormal movement or signs of displacement.
Stretcher is placed for optimal viewing during ambulance travel and offloading.
All systems are safe and secured. We're proceeding to VCU ED now.
Rate of transport is steady and secure to ensure no further harm.
Scene was left without incident and from a clean, controlled environment.
Patient body alignment was supported on board the ambulance bench with minimal displacement risk.
In medical mode with fast transport via streets with appropriate clearance notified.
She met the tox or psychiatry evaluation criteria, but transport to ED is more immediate.
Handover was completed and signed off by ED staff present.
VCU staff has confirmed arrival and taken full responsibility for patient care now.
Final pulse is regular at about 120 beats per minute.
